B) reasoning that a claim is true because it hasn't been disproven. (This is related to "appeal to ignorance," not frequency.)
C) arguing that an act should be carried out because it will not have any significant consequences, when there is no evidence of the effects. (This is related to "appeal to consequence," not frequency.)

E) arguing that something should be done because that's the way it's been done in the past. (This is "appeal to tradition.")
F) reasoning that a conclusion is true because it's been claimed so often. (This matches the definition of "ad nauseam" - assuming truth based on frequency of claim.)
G) reasoning that a claim is false because so many people believe it is false. (This is "appeal to popularity" or "appeal to the people.")
H) arguing that an act should be avoided because it will have significant consequences, when there is no evidence of the effects. (This is related to "appeal to consequence.")
I) reasoning that a claim is true because so many people believe it is true. (This is "appeal to popularity.")
J) arguing that something should not be done because it's never been done in the past. (This is "appeal to tradition" in reverse.)

Step 4: Select the best match.
The "ad nauseam fallacy" is defined as assuming a conclusion is true because it has been claimed so often. This matches Option F.
